component_identification.content: Rulebook contents: 7 Wonder boards (double-sided A/B), 7 Wonder cards, 49 Age I cards, 49 Age II cards, 50 Age III cards, 46 conflict tokens (defeat -1 and victory +1/+3/+5), coins (46 value-1 and 24 value-3 in the listed edition), score booklet, and rulebook, plus 2 cards for 2-player variant. Core card colors are brown/gray resources, yellow commerce, red military, blue civic, green science, and purple guilds (Age III subset). Each player begins with one Wonder board that grants a starting resource and stage tracks with costs and rewards.

core_game_loop.content: 7 Wonders is played over 3 Ages. In each Age, every player receives a hand, chooses one card simultaneously, then reveals together and resolves the chosen card by either building it, using it to build a Wonder stage, or discarding it for 3 coins. After resolution, each player passes their remaining hand left or right depending on age direction. This continues until six cards are played and the final card is discarded. At each Age end, resolve military conflicts with left and right neighbors, then begin the next Age with a fresh deck.

detailed_rules.content: To build a card, pay its printed resource/coin cost unless it is free by chain symbol. Resource production from brown/gray cards is persistent and can be used each turn for your own builds. If short on resources, you may buy resources only from immediate neighbors (left and/or right), paying coins to the supplying neighbor; by default this is 2 coins per purchased resource, modified by yellow discount cards. You cannot buy from non-neighbors. Building a Wonder stage uses one selected card face down under the current stage, paying the stage’s listed cost and gaining its benefit. Military is checked at end of each age: compare shield totals with each neighbor separately; gain age-appropriate victory token for each win and a -1 token for each loss. Science uses three symbols and scores by both sets and symbol concentration; this is often mis-scored, so calculate carefully. Purple guild cards appear in Age III and generally score from your neighbors’ tableau composition. Coin economy matters all game: coins are needed for trading and some card costs, and leftover coins convert to points at game end. Key beginner edge cases: a free-chain build ignores normal resource cost entirely, neighbors do not lose resource production when selling to you, and each military comparison is independent (you can beat one side and lose the other). Teaching clarifications from rulebook-first play: always resolve effects in printed timing windows and finish mandatory steps before optional effects. If a card or tile text conflicts with a baseline rule, the specific card text takes priority for that interaction only. During teaching, call out hidden information boundaries, legal action checks before payment, and tie handling exactly as written. Most play disputes come from skipped sequence steps, so keep turn order visible and confirm each phase aloud in early rounds.

scoring_and_endgame.content: After Age III military resolution, total points from all categories: blue civic structures, red military tokens, green science, yellow commerce points, purple guild points, Wonder-stage points, and coin conversion at 3 coins = 1 point (rounded down). Highest score wins; tie-breaker is most coins. If still tied, tied players share victory per common rules convention. Ensure science is calculated with both set bonuses and square-value symbol counts.

beginner_strategy.content: In Age I, secure production first: a city with weak raw resources is forced into expensive trading all game. Track both neighbors constantly because they determine your military matchups, your trade options, and your guild value ceiling in Age III. Use chain symbols deliberately—planning one or two free upgrades can swing tempo and coin efficiency. Don’t overforce science without access; partial science plans often underperform a stable civic/military economy. Build Wonder stages opportunistically when your current hand is weak or when the stage reward is immediate tempo (coins, shields, extra turns/effects depending on board side). In Age III, choose between point maximization and denial drafting: sometimes taking a mediocre card is correct if it blocks a huge guild/science payoff for a neighbor. New-player coaching tip: choose one clear plan by the end of early game, then spend midgame turns increasing action efficiency rather than collecting disconnected benefits. Track remaining rounds/end triggers constantly; many losses come from mis-timing rather than bad tactical choices. When uncertain between two similar options, prefer the move that preserves flexibility for the next turn while still scoring now.

metadata.notes: Rewrite sprint using official rulebook text as primary reference.
